title: Quiz: Global Optimization Techniques
description: Test your understanding of global circuit optimization and mapping-aware techniques

questions.question: What is the main limitation of full unitary resynthesis optimization?

options.text: It only works on single-qubit gates
The unitary matrix size grows exponentially with qubit count (2^n x 2^n)
It cannot handle parameterized gates
It requires quantum hardware to execute

questions.explanation: The unitary matrix for n qubits is 2^n x 2^n, which becomes impractically large for more than about 5-6 qubits. This exponential growth makes full resynthesis infeasible for larger circuits.

questions.question: In topology-aware optimization, what determines whether two qubits can directly interact?

options.text: Their logical qubit numbers
The hardware's physical connectivity graph
The type of gates being used
The circuit depth

questions.explanation: Hardware topology defines which physical qubits are connected and can directly interact via two-qubit gates. Non-adjacent qubits require SWAP operations to bring them together.

questions.question: Why is initial qubit mapping important for optimization?

options.text: It determines the final measurement results
A good mapping reduces the number of SWAP gates needed for routing
It only affects single-qubit gate performance
Initial mapping has no impact on final circuit quality

questions.explanation: Good initial mapping places frequently-interacting logical qubits on connected physical qubits, minimizing the SWAP operations needed during routing. This significantly reduces gate count and circuit depth.

questions.question: ASAP (As Soon As Possible) scheduling always minimizes circuit depth.

options.text: True
False

questions.explanation: ASAP scheduling places each gate at the earliest possible time step (immediately after its dependencies complete), which by definition produces the minimum possible circuit depth for a given gate ordering.

questions.question: What is the trade-off between optimizing for gate count vs. circuit depth?

options.text: They are always optimized together
Reducing gate count may increase depth (less parallelism), and vice versa
Gate count is always more important than depth
Circuit depth cannot be optimized independently

questions.explanation: Gate count and depth can be conflicting objectives. For example, gate merging might reduce count but serialize operations, increasing depth. Depth reduction might require duplicating gates for parallelism. The best choice depends on hardware characteristics.

questions.question: In routing, what is the purpose of inserting SWAP gates?

options.text: To perform computations faster
To move logical qubit states between physical qubits, enabling non-adjacent interactions
To reduce circuit depth
To implement error correction

questions.explanation: SWAP gates exchange qubit states between physical locations. When a two-qubit gate requires qubits that are not physically connected, SWAPs move one or both qubit states to adjacent positions where the interaction can occur.
